hook: You already know how to identify angle relationships when parallel lines are cut by a transversal — corresponding angles are equal, alternate interior angles are equal, and co-interior angles are supplementary. But what if nobody tells you the lines are parallel? Suppose you're handed two lines and a transversal with some angle measures labeled — can you use those angle relationships in reverse to prove the lines must be parallel? That's the challenge: working backward from angle evidence to a conclusion about the lines themselves.
concept: Proving lines parallel means using angle relationships as evidence rather than as consequences. Formally, if two lines are cut by a transversal and either (1) a pair of corresponding angles are congruent, (2) alternate interior angles are congruent, (3) alternate exterior angles are congruent, or (4) co-interior (same-side interior) angles are supplementary ($m\angle A + m\angle B = 180^\circ$), then the two lines are parallel — each condition is a theorem that acts as a sufficient criterion for parallelism.
anatomy: Every parallel-lines proof built on a transversal has four key components. First, the two lines being tested (call them $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$) are the subjects of your conclusion. Second, the transversal $t$ is the line that crosses both, creating eight angles at two intersection points. Third, the angle pair is the specific pair of angles you are analyzing — identified by their position (corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior, or co-interior). Fourth, the converse theorem is the named rule you invoke: for example, the Converse of the Corresponding Angles Theorem states that if corresponding angles are congruent, then $\ell_1 \parallel \ell_2$.

worked_example.description_latex: We are given that angle 3 and angle 7 each measure $65^\circ$, and they are formed where a transversal crosses two lines. Before invoking any theorem, we need to identify the relationship between these two angles — look at their positions: angle 3 is above the first line on the right side of the transversal, and angle 7 is above the second line on the right side of the transversal, placing them in corresponding positions.

Because both angles occupy the same position (above the intersection, to the right of the transversal) at each of the two intersections, they are corresponding angles. Correctly naming the angle relationship is critical because it determines which converse theorem you are allowed to use.

Since both angles measure $65^\circ$, they are congruent. We write this as $\angle 3 \cong \angle 7$ to set up the exact hypothesis required by our theorem — the theorem needs congruent corresponding angles, not just equal numbers, so stating congruence formally completes that hypothesis.

We conclude that the two lines are parallel by citing the Converse of the Corresponding Angles Theorem: if two lines are cut by a transversal so that corresponding angles are congruent, then the lines are parallel. Always name the theorem explicitly — a conclusion without justification is not a proof.

partial_example.description_latex: We are given two angle measures formed by a transversal cutting two lines. Angle 4 is below the first intersection on the right of the transversal, and angle 5 is above the second intersection on the left — identify the type of angle pair these represent before choosing a theorem.

Classify this angle pair by name. Are these corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior, or co-interior angles? State the classification and explain why their position between the two lines and on opposite sides of the transversal matters.
Because both angles measure $112^\circ$ they are congruent, satisfying the hypothesis of the theorem we will apply. Now you have everything needed to write the conclusion.
Write the conclusion: state whether the lines are parallel and cite the specific converse theorem by name that justifies your conclusion based on the angle pair you identified above.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Two lines are cut by a transversal. The corresponding angles measure $74^\circ$ each. Are the lines parallel? State your reason.
practice_problems.answer_latex: Yes, \ell_1 \parallel \ell_2 \text{ by the Converse of the Corresponding Angles Theorem.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A transversal crosses two lines forming alternate interior angles that measure $53^\circ$ and $53^\circ$. Prove the lines are parallel.
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{The alternate interior angles are congruent, so } \ell_1 \parallel \ell_2 \text{ by the Converse of the Alternate Interior Angles Theorem.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Two lines are cut by a transversal. Co-interior (same-side interior) angles measure $(3x + 15)^\circ$ and $(2x + 25)^\circ$. Find $x$ so that the lines are parallel.

In a diagram, $\overrightarrow{AB}$ and $\overrightarrow{CD}$ are cut by transversal $\overrightarrow{EF}$. You know $m\angle 1 = 4x - 10$ and $m\angle 2 = 3x + 20$ where $\angle 1$ and $\angle 2$ are alternate exterior angles. Determine the value of $x$ that makes $\overrightarrow{AB} \parallel \overrightarrow{CD}$, then verify by finding each angle measure.

A carpenter checks two wall studs with a level board acting as a transversal. She measures the co-interior angles and finds one is $88^\circ$ and the other is $95^\circ$. Are the studs parallel? Explain what the angle sum tells her.
practice_problems.answer_latex: 88+95=183^\circ \neq 180^\circ, \text{ so the studs are NOT parallel; co-interior angles must sum to } 180^\circ \text{ for parallelism.}

common_mistakes: Students apply the original theorem (parallel lines produce congruent angles) instead of its converse (congruent angles prove lines parallel), reversing the logical direction — remember, here the angle fact is your evidence and parallel lines is your conclusion, not the other way around.

Students mislabel co-interior angles as alternate interior angles because both pairs sit between the two lines; the critical difference is that co-interior angles are on the SAME side of the transversal, so they must be supplementary (not congruent) to prove parallelism — always check which side of the transversal each angle is on before naming the pair.

Students forget to cite the specific converse theorem by name in their proof, writing only 'the lines are parallel' without justification; every proof step must reference the theorem that licenses the conclusion, because a statement without a reason is not accepted as a valid proof.
untested_variants: Problems where three or more lines are cut by a single transversal and students must determine which pairs (if any) are parallel based on multiple angle conditions simultaneously.
Algebraic problems where the angle expressions involve two variables, requiring a system of equations before the parallel condition can be applied.
Coordinate geometry versions where students must compute slopes of two lines and use the equal-slopes criterion ($m_1 = m_2$) as the algebraic equivalent of the parallel-lines theorems.

Proof problems written in two-column or paragraph format that require students to chain multiple converse theorems together — for example, first proving two lines parallel and then using that result as a given to find an unknown angle in a larger figure.
